one_line_takeaway: VLMs can reason well about laboratory hazards when given structured (scene-graph) text, but perform far worse reasoning directly from images-a gap the authors partially close by prompting models to first self-generate a scene graph from the image before judging hazards, though this introduces new parsing failures and the paper is about physical lab-safety monitoring, not AI/cyber security.
key_finding: Across all seven VLMs, textual scene-graph input (TSG+H or TSG-H) yields the highest F1 scores, vision-only (V) input yields the worst, and adding a textual scene graph to the image (V+TSG) sits in between-improving over vision-only in most cases. Concrete numbers from Table 1 (overall F1): vision-only F1 ranges from 0.0 (LLaVA-NeXT-7B, which predicts nearly everything non-hazardous) up to 0.86 (Gemini-3-Pro) and 0.85 (Gemini-3-Flash); LLaMA-3.2-11B and Mistral-medium-3 score 0.43 and 0.61 respectively vision-only, with LLaMA showing an extremely high parsing-error rate (58.28% overall, up to 75.94% for Cryogenic Liquids). With TSG-H (text scene graph, hazard attribute removed) the same models reach F1 of 0.88-0.96 (e.g., LLaMA jumps from 0.43 to 0.88, Mistral from 0.61 to 0.92, Gemini-3-Pro from 0.86 to 0.96). The proposed SG-guided (SG-G) approach, applied only in the visual-only setting, improves F1 over plain vision-only for most models (e.g., LLaVA 0.0 to 0.22, LLaMA 0.43 to 0.71, Qwen3 0.68 to 0.78, Mistral 0.61 to 0.69) though it does not close the gap to text-scene-graph performance and for some strong models (Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Gemini-3-Pro) SG-G is roughly flat or slightly worse than vision-only. The hazard-attribute ablation (Figure 6/Table 1, TSG+H vs TSG-H) shows explicit hazard attributes help the smallest model (LLaVA, whose F1 improves with hazard attributes present) but hurt or barely change performance for larger models, which the authors interpret as evidence that larger models rely on hazard-attribute shortcuts rather than reasoning over object states/relationships when attributes are given, and reason better when forced to infer hazards from state/relationship structure alone. MAE for hazard count remains comparatively high across settings (e.g., in TSG+H settings MAE is often 0.4-1.4) even where classification F1 is strong, indicating hazard counting/localization is harder than binary detection. Subject-wise, Cryogenic Liquids and Physics are the hardest domains in the vision-only setting (Figure 7).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is threefold: (1) a structured synthetic data-generation pipeline in which an LLM (GPT-5) acts as an 'architect' that converts textual laboratory hazard scenarios from LabSafetyBench into Visual-Genome-style scene graphs (objects with State/Hazard attributes plus subject-predicate-object relation triples), and a text-to-image model (Google's Nano-banana-pro) acts as a 'renderer' that synthesizes photorealistic laboratory images conditioned on those scene graphs, producing a final aligned dataset of 1,207 <image, scene graph, ground-truth> triples spanning 362 unique scenarios (from an original 394 re-annotated scenarios / 1,970 generated candidate images, filtered via a VLM-as-judge alignment check); (2) a systematic benchmark of seven open- and closed-source VLMs (LLaVA-NeXT-7B, Qwen3-8B, LLaMA-3.2-11B-Vision, Mistral-medium-3, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Gemini-3-Flash, Gemini-3-Pro) for binary hazard classification and hazard counting across four input settings: vision-only (V), textual scene graph with hazard attributes (TSG+H), textual scene graph without hazard attributes (TSG-H), and vision+textual-scene-graph (V+TSG); and (3) a post-training, context-engineering method called 'scene-graph-guided alignment' (SG-G) in which a VLM is prompted to first reconstruct a scene graph from the raw image and then perform hazard reasoning over that self-generated graph, rather than reasoning directly over pixels.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a physical/occupational laboratory-safety monitoring paper (detecting things like missing PPE, chemical spills, unsafe electrical/chemical storage from camera images), not an AI-security, adversarial-robustness, or cybersecurity paper. There is no adversary, attack surface, or security threat model in the paper; it should not be forced into a security-KG threat-model field. The closest 'safety' concept discussed is physical-lab hazard detection, not model safety/alignment/jailbreak security.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) VLM-as-judge filtering introduces selection bias-Figure 8 shows cases (e.g., torn gloves, chemical spill) where the judge incorrectly rejects genuinely aligned images due to limited sensitivity to fine-grained visual detail, discarding hard-but-informative samples and reducing dataset diversity; the authors note ongoing human review to recover these. (2) Smaller/resource-efficient models-the ones most likely to be deployed in real labs-perform worst in the vision-only setting: LLaVA-NeXT-7B has an F1 of exactly 0, Qwen3-8B is high-precision/low-recall (overly conservative), and LLaMA-3.2-11B has a high parsing-error rate, indicating current models lack sufficient task-specific visual reasoning for reliable direct-from-pixels hazard detection. (3) SG-guided reasoning has a specific failure mode: it increases parsing-error rate because models often fail to progress past the scene-graph-generation stage to reach hazard classification, revealing a bottleneck in the reliability of vision-to-graph generation itself. (4) The dataset is entirely synthetic (LLM-authored scenarios and AI-generated images), not real laboratory camera footage, so ecological validity to real deployments is untested. (5) The paper's own conclusion frames SG-guided alignment as a post-training/context-engineering (prompting) technique rather than a trained capability, and calls for future instruction-tuning to have models internalize scene-graph generation and hazard reasoning jointly.

datasets_used: LabSafetyBench (Zhou et al., 2026) - source of 404 original textual laboratory safety scenarios, re-annotated by the authors

Authors' own synthetic dataset: 394 re-annotated scenarios (230 non-hazardous, 164 hazardous) -> 1,970 candidate generated images (5 per scenario) -> 1,207 final aligned <image, scene graph, ground truth> triples across 362 unique scenarios (37.4% hazardous, 62.6% non-hazardous) after VLM-as-judge + human filtering
quantitative_results: Human-human ground-truth relabeling agreement: Cohen's Kappa = 1.0 (404->394 scenarios)
VLM-judge vs human agreement: Gemini-3-Pro 48.1%, GPT-5.2-Pro 16.2% (Gemini-3-Pro selected as judge)
Retained 1,207 of 1,970 generated images after judge filtering
Overall F1 (vision-only): LLaVA-NeXT-7B 0.0, Qwen3-8B 0.68, LLaMA-3.2-11B 0.43, Mistral-medium-3 0.61, Claude-Sonnet-4.5 0.62, Gemini-3-Flash 0.85, Gemini-3-Pro 0.86
Overall F1 (TSG-H, text scene graph w/o hazard attribute): LLaVA 0.54, Qwen3 0.93, LLaMA 0.88, Mistral 0.92, Claude 0.94, Gemini-3-Flash 0.96, Gemini-3-Pro 0.96
Overall F1 (SG-guided, vision-only + self-generated scene graph): LLaVA 0.22, Qwen3 0.78, LLaMA 0.71, Mistral 0.69, Claude 0.70, Gemini-3-Flash 0.85, Gemini-3-Pro 0.85
LLaMA-3.2-11B parsing-error rate: 58.28% overall (vision-only), rising to 75.94% for Cryogenic Liquids subject
LLaMA-3.2-11B parsing-error rate under SG-G: 97.1% overall
Overall MAE for hazard count under TSG+H ranges roughly 0.13-1.35 across models (e.g., LLaMA 1.35, Claude 0.23, Gemini-3-Pro 0.13)
baselines_compared: Compared across 4 input/reasoning settings: vision-only (V), textual scene graph with hazard attribute (TSG+H), textual scene graph without hazard attribute (TSG-H), vision+textual-scene-graph (V+TSG), and the proposed SG-guided (SG-G) visual-only variant
